sections.about: This metric measures the total number of workers disclosed at financial year-end, including permanent workers and contract or third-party workers engaged in the entity's operations. It falls under BRSR Section A — General Disclosures and is distinct from the employee headcount; together they reflect the full direct and contracted workforce. No direction is assigned: scale, insourcing strategy and contractor intensity all influence the count.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: SAIL reports the largest permanent worker base in FY2024-25 at 73,819, followed by Tata Steel (India entities) at 31,794, JSW at 25,047 and Jindal at 13,529. Tata's permanent worker count under the India entities scope grew from 30,486 in FY2022-23 to 31,794 in FY2024-25. SAIL's leading position reflects its large public-sector integrated operations spanning multiple integrated steel plants and operating mines. JSW and Jindal both showed increases over the period, with JSW growing from 17,981 to 25,047 and Jindal from 10,577 to 13,529.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata (India entities) discloses 31,794 permanent workers — second in the peer set, behind SAIL at 73,819. Tata's permanent worker count under the India entities scope is more directly comparable with peers than the previously cited consolidated figure.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel data covers Tata Steel entities in India (permanent workers) across all years and is not strictly standalone (Tata Steel Limited only) or full consolidated (which includes overseas operations). All peers report on a standalone basis. Comparisons reflect permanent workers at respective India operations for Tata versus standalone for peers.
